Article.ArticleTitle: 24 h severe fluid restriction increases a biomarker of renal injury in healthy males.

Abstract.AbstractText: Exercise-induced hypohydration exacerbates biomarkers of renal injury, but studies isolating the effects of hypohydration without exercise have produced mixed findings. This study investigated the effects of 24-h severe fluid restriction on biomarkers of renal injury and glucose tolerance.

Fifteen males (age: 27 ± 5 y; BMI: 24.1 ± 3.8 kg/m2) completed two randomised trials, involving consuming either 40 mL/kg body mass water to maintain euhydration (EU) or severe fluid restriction via limiting water consumption to 100 mL (HYP). A standardised dry food diet was consumed in both trials (~ 300 g water). At baseline and 24 h post-baseline, nude body mass, and blood and urine samples (additional urine sample at 12 h) were collected. An oral glucose tolerance test was conducted after 24-h post-baseline measurements (n = 12).

At 24 h, body mass loss (HYP: - 1.52 ± 0.34%, EU: - 0.24 ± 0.40%), plasma volume loss, serum, and urine osmolality were greater in HYP than EU (P ≤ 0.004). Osmolality-corrected urinary kidney injury molecule-1 (uKIM-1) concentrations were greater in HYP at 12 (HYP: 1.097 ± 0.587 ng/mOsm, EU: 0.570 ± 0.408 ng/mOsm; P < 0.001) and 24-h (HYP: 1.932 ± 1.173 ng/mOsm, EU: 1.599 ± 1.012 ng/mOsm; P = 0.01). There was no trial-by-time interactions for osmolality-corrected urinary neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin concentrations (P = 0.781) or plasma glucose (P = 0.550) and insulin (P = 0.193) concentrations.
Hypohydration produced by 24-h fluid restriction increased proximal tubular injury but did not affect glucose tolerance.

School of Sport, Exercise and Health Sciences, Loughborough University, Leicestershire, UK. S.A.Mears@lboro.ac.uk.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declarations. Conflict of interest: LAJ has previously had conference fees covered by Danone Nutricia. MPF is funded by the National Institute for Health Research (NIHR) Applied Research Collaboration East Midlands (ARC EM). The views expressed are those of the author and not necessarily those of the NIHR, NHS, or the Department of Health and Social Care. HAC has received research funding from the Economic and Social Research Council, the European Hydration Institute, and currently from the Esther Olssons stiftelse II & Anna Jönssons Minnesfond; has conducted research for Tate & Lyle; and has received speakers’ fees from Danone Nutricia Research; and has received conference, travel, and accommodation fees from Danone Nutricia Research. LJJ is part of the National Institute for Health Research’s Leicester Biomedical Research Centre, which is a partnership between University Hospitals of Leicester NHS Trust, Loughborough University, and the University of Leicester. This is a summary of independent research funded by Loughborough University and carried out at the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR) Leicester Biomedical Research Centre (BRC). The views expressed are those of the author(s) and not necessarily those of the NIHR or the Department of Health and Social Care. LJJ has current/previous funding from Entrinsic Beverage Company LLC, Entrinsic Bioscience LLC, Herbalife Europe Ltd, Bridge Farm Nurseries, Decathlon SA, PepsiCo Inc., Volac International; has performed consultancy for PepsiCo Inc. and Lucozade, Ribena Suntory; and has received conference fees from PepsiCo Inc. and Danone Nutricia. In all cases, monies have been paid to LJJs institution and not directly to LJJ. SAM has current/previous funding from Entrinsic Beverage Company LLP and Herbalife Europe Ltd. The authors declare no other conflict of interest. Ethical approval: The Loughborough University Ethics Approvals (Human Participants) Sub-Committee granted this study full ethical approval (Reference: R18-P144) and therefore performed in accordance with the ethical standards laid down in the 1964 Declaration of Helsinki and all subsequent amendments. All participants provided verbal and written informed consent before inclusion in the study.
